content:
# Acts Chapter 28 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 260-261 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_028_page_001.jpg, ACT_028_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And having been brought safely through, we then learned that the island was called Malta.

**2** The native people showed us no ordinary kindness, for having kindled a fire, they welcomed us all, on account of the rain that had set in and on account of the cold.

**3** Now when Paul had gathered a bundle of sticks and laid them on the fire, a viper came out because of the heat and fastened itself onto his hand.

**4** And when the native people saw the creature hanging from his hand, they said to one another, "Surely this man is a murderer, whom, though he was saved from the sea, Justice has not allowed to live."

**5** But he shook off the creature into the fire and suffered no harm.

**6** But they were expecting him to swell up or suddenly to fall down dead. And when they had waited a long time and saw nothing unusual happening to him, they changed their minds and said that he was a god.

**7** Now in the region around that place were lands belonging to the chief man of the island, named Publius, who received us and hospitably entertained us for three days.

**8** And it happened that the father of Publius lay sick with fever and dysentery. Paul went in to him, and having prayed and laid his hands on him, healed him.

**9** And when this had taken place, the rest of those on the island who had diseases also came and were healed.

**10** They also honored us with many honors, and when we were setting sail, they loaded us with the things we needed.

**11** And after three months we set sail in an Alexandrian ship that had wintered at the island, with the sign of the Dioscuri.

**12** And having put in at Syracuse, we remained there three days.

**13** From there we sailed around and arrived at Rhegium. And after one day a south wind sprang up, and on the second day we came to Puteoli,

**14** where we found brothers and were urged to remain with them seven days. And so we came to Rome.

**15** And from there the brothers, having heard about us, came to meet us as far as the Forum of Appius and the Three Taverns. When Paul saw them, he gave thanks to God and took courage.

**16** And when we entered Rome, Paul was permitted to stay by himself, with the soldier who was guarding him.

**17** And it came to pass that after three days he called together the leading men of the Jews. And when they had assembled, he said to them, "Brothers, though I had done nothing against our people or the customs of our fathers, I was delivered as a prisoner from Jerusalem into the hands of the Romans,

**18** who, having examined me, wished to release me, because there was no cause for a death sentence against me.

**19** But when the Jews objected, I was compelled to appeal to Caesar -- not as though I had any accusation to bring against my own nation.

**20** For this reason, therefore, I have called for you, to see you and to speak with you, for it is on account of the hope of Israel that I am bound with this chain."

**21** And they said to him, "We have received no letters from Judea concerning you, nor has any of the brothers who arrived reported or spoken anything evil about you.

**22** But we think it fitting to hear from you what you think, for concerning this sect, it is known to us that everywhere it is spoken against."

**23** And having appointed a day for him, they came to him at his lodging in greater numbers. He expounded the matter to them, testifying to the kingdom of God and persuading them concerning Jesus, both from the Law of Moses and from the Prophets, from morning until evening.

**24** And some were persuaded by what was said, but others disbelieved.

**25** And being in disagreement with one another, they began to depart, after Paul had spoken one word: "Rightly did the Holy Spirit speak through Isaiah the prophet to your fathers,

**26** saying, 'Go to this people and say: By hearing you will hear and will by no means understand, and seeing you will see and will by no means perceive.

**27** For the heart of this people has grown dull, and with their ears they have heard heavily, and their eyes they have closed, lest they should see with their eyes and hear with their ears and understand with their heart and turn, and I would heal them.'

**28** Let it be known to you, therefore, that this salvation of God has been sent to the Gentiles; they indeed will listen."

**29** [And when he had said these things, the Jews departed, having much dispute among themselves.]

**30** And he remained two whole years in his own rented dwelling, and he welcomed all who came to him,

**31** proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching the things concerning the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness, unhindered.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

- **Verse 11:** All three translators note the Dioscuri (Castor and Pollux) as patron deities of sailors. SOPHIA retains the transliterated proper noun "Dioscuri" as EMET and LOGOS do, since it is a proper name.
- **Verse 16:** All three translators correctly follow Sinaiticus in omitting the Western reading about the centurion delivering prisoners to the camp commander. The shorter Alexandrian text is unanimous.
- **Verse 26-27:** All three translators render the Isaiah 6:9-10 quotation in substantially the same manner, following the Septuagint text preserved in Sinaiticus.
- **Verse 29:** All three translators correctly bracket this verse as absent from Sinaiticus and the earliest witnesses. SOPHIA follows this consensus.
- **Verse 31 -- AKOLUTOS:** All three translators agree that this emphatic final word of Acts carries the theological weight of Luke's conclusion: the gospel advances unhindered. SOPHIA preserves "unhindered" as the closing word of the book.
- **Subscription:** The manuscript page (page 2) shows PRAXEIS APOSTOLON ("Acts of the Apostles") after verse 31, confirming the end of the book.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | Phrasing of safe arrival | "having-been-brought-safely-through, then we recognized" | "having been brought safely through, we then learned" | "Once we had made it safely to shore, we learned" | Adopted LOGOS: "having been brought safely through, we then learned" -- preserves the Greek participle while being readable. PNEUMA's "made it safely to shore" is interpretive expansion. |
| 1 | Verb for discovery | "recognized" (epegnomen) | "learned" | "learned" | "learned" -- two-to-one; both are valid for epiginoskein, but "learned" suits the context of discovering the island's name. |
| 2 | Description of natives | "barbarians [barbaroi]" | "native people" | "local people" | "native people" (LOGOS) -- avoids modern pejorative connotation of "barbarian" while remaining formal. EMET's transliteration is noted but not adopted for readability. |
| 2 | Description of kindness | "no ordinary kindness" | "no ordinary kindness" | "extraordinary kindness" | "no ordinary kindness" -- two-to-one; more literal rendering of ou tychousan. |
| 2 | Verb for receiving | "took-us-all-in" | "received us all" | "welcomed every one of us" | "welcomed us all" -- blends LOGOS's accuracy with PNEUMA's warmth; proselabonto carries a sense of warm reception. |
| 3 | Description of wood gathered | "a certain bundle of sticks" | "a bundle of sticks" | "an armful of brushwood" | "a bundle of sticks" (LOGOS) -- accurate without EMET's over-literal "certain" or PNEUMA's interpretive "armful of brushwood." |
| 3 | Syntax of viper's appearance | "a viper, having-come-out from the heat, fastened-onto his hand" | "a viper came out because of the heat and fastened itself upon his hand" | "a viper, driven out by the heat, fastened itself onto his hand" | Adopted LOGOS's clear sequential rendering. PNEUMA's "driven out" is interpretive; the Greek says "coming out from" (exelthein apo). |
| 4 | Identification of speakers | "barbarians [barbaroi]" | "native people" | "islanders" | "native people" -- consistent with v. 2 decision. |
| 4 | Opening of their speech | "By-all-means this man is a murderer" | "Surely this man is a murderer" | "This man must be a murderer" | "Surely this man is a murderer" (LOGOS) -- pantos is an emphatic particle; "surely" captures it formally without EMET's awkward hyphenation or PNEUMA's paraphrase. |
| 4 | "saved from the sea" phrasing | "having-been-saved out of the sea" | "though he has been saved from the sea" | "He survived the sea" | "though he was saved from the sea" -- follows LOGOS's concessive sense while simplifying the tense. PNEUMA's "survived" loses the soteriological nuance of diasothenta. |
| 5 | Subject identification | "He indeed therefore" | "He, however" | "But Paul" | "But he" -- the Greek uses the pronoun (ho men oun); PNEUMA's addition of "Paul" is not in the text. LOGOS's "however" captures the contrast well. |
| 5 | Description of outcome | "suffered nothing evil" | "suffered no harm" | "was completely unharmed" | "suffered no harm" (LOGOS) -- accurate to epathen ouden kakon. PNEUMA's "completely unharmed" adds emphasis not in the Greek. |
| 6 | Structure of expectation clause | "they were expecting him to be about to swell-up or to fall-down suddenly dead" | "they were expecting him to swell up or suddenly to fall down dead" | "They kept watching, expecting him to swell up or suddenly drop dead" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "kept watching" and "drop dead" are interpretive additions. |
| 6 | Phrasing of changed opinion | "having-changed-their-minds, they were saying him to be a god" | "they changed their minds and said that he was a god" | "they changed their minds and began saying he was a god" | "they changed their minds and said that he was a god" (LOGOS) -- PNEUMA's "began saying" adds inceptive aspect not explicit in the Greek. |
| 7 | Description of location | "in the [places] around that place were lands belonging-to the chief-man of the island" | "in the region around that place were lands belonging to the chief man of the island" | "Near that place were estates belonging to the leading man of the island" | Adopted LOGOS: "in the region around that place were lands belonging to the chief man of the island." PNEUMA's "estates" is interpretive; "lands" (choria) is more accurate. |
| 7 | Name rendering | "Poplius [Publius]" | "Publius" | "Publius" | "Publius" -- the standard English form. EMET's preservation of the Sinaiticus spelling is noted but unnecessary for the consensus text. |
| 7 | Description of hospitality | "hospitably lodged [us]" | "hospitably entertained us" | "hosted us generously" | "hospitably entertained us" (LOGOS) -- philophronos is "hospitably/courteously"; exenisen means "hosted/entertained." |
| 8 | Description of illness | "being-oppressed-by fevers and dysentery, was lying [in bed]" | "lay sick with fever and dysentery" | "was lying sick with fever and dysentery" | "lay sick with fever and dysentery" (LOGOS) -- clean and accurate. EMET's "being-oppressed-by" over-translates synechomenon. |
| 8 | Description of Paul's actions | "having-entered and having-prayed, having-laid-on his hands, healed him" | "went in to him, and having prayed and laid his hands on him, healed him" | "went in to see him, prayed, placed his hands on him, and healed him" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "to see him" is an addition. EMET's participle chains are too heavy for English. |
| 9 | Phrasing | "this having-happened, also the rest, the ones in the island having sicknesses, were coming and were being healed" | "when this had taken place, the rest of those on the island who had diseases also came and were healed" | "After that, the rest of the people on the island who had diseases also came and were cured" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "cured" for etherapeuonto is acceptable but "healed" is more consistent with v. 8. |
| 10 | "honors" vs "gifts" | "honored us with many honors" | "honored us with many honors" | "honored us with many gifts" | "honored us with many honors" -- two-to-one; the Greek is timai (honors), not dorea (gifts). PNEUMA's "gifts" is interpretive. |
| 10 | Sailing provision phrasing | "upon [our] setting-sail, they placed-upon [us] the things for [our] needs" | "when we were setting sail, they loaded us with the things we needed" | "when we were ready to sail, they loaded us up with everything we needed" | "when we were setting sail, they loaded us with the things we needed" (LOGOS) -- PNEUMA's "ready to sail" and "everything" are slight expansions. |
| 11 | Ship description order | "in a ship having-wintered in the island, an Alexandrian [ship], with-the-sign Dioscuri" | "in a ship that had wintered at the island, an Alexandrian ship, with the sign of the Dioscuri" | "in an Alexandrian ship that had wintered at the island, one with the Twin Brothers as its figurehead" | Adopted LOGOS's word order, which follows the Greek. PNEUMA's "Twin Brothers as its figurehead" is explanatory rather than translational. |
| 12 | Phrasing | "having-put-in at Syrakousai [Syracuse], we remained three days" | "having put in at Syracuse, we remained there three days" | "We put in at Syracuse and stayed there three days" | "having put in at Syracuse, we remained there three days" (LOGOS) -- preserves the participial structure. |
| 13 | Coastal navigation term | "having-gone-around" (perielthontes) | "we sailed around" | "we followed the coast" | "we sailed around" (LOGOS) -- perielthontes means "having gone around," and LOGOS renders it naturally. PNEUMA's "followed the coast" is interpretive. |
| 13 | South wind phrasing | "a south-wind having-come-on" | "a south wind sprang up" | "a south wind came up" | "a south wind sprang up" (LOGOS) -- epigenomenou suggests a sudden onset; "sprang up" captures this well. |
| 13 | Final verb | "we came to Potiolous [Puteoli]" | "we came to Puteoli" | "we reached Puteoli" | "we came to Puteoli" (LOGOS) -- elthomen is simply "we came." |
| 14 | "brothers" vs "fellow believers" | "brothers [adelphous]" | "brothers" | "fellow believers" | "brothers" -- two-to-one; adelphous is literally "brothers." PNEUMA's "fellow believers" is interpretive, though contextually correct. |
| 14 | "entreated" vs "urged" | "were entreated to remain" | "were urged to remain" | "urged us to stay" | "were urged to remain" (LOGOS) -- paraklethemen is "we were urged/entreated." |
| 14 | Final clause | "And thus to Rome we came" | "And so we came to Rome" | "And so we came toward Rome" | "And so we came to Rome" (LOGOS) -- PNEUMA's "toward Rome" (eis with accusative can mean "to" or "toward") is unusual; the arrival is stated. |
| 15 | "brothers" rendering | "brothers [adelphoi]" | "brothers" | "brothers and sisters" | "brothers" -- two-to-one; the Greek is adelphoi. PNEUMA's inclusive "brothers and sisters" is an interpretive choice not supported by the manuscript text. |
| 15 | Paul's response | "Paul, having-given-thanks to God, took courage" | "he gave thanks to God and took courage" | "he thanked God and took courage" | "he gave thanks to God and took courage" (LOGOS) -- eucharistesas is well rendered by "gave thanks"; PNEUMA's "thanked" is acceptable but less formal. |
| 16 | Verb for entering Rome | "when we entered into Rome" | "when we entered Rome" | "When we arrived in Rome" | "when we entered Rome" (LOGOS) -- eiselthein is "to enter." PNEUMA's "arrived" is less precise. |
| 16 | Paul's living arrangement | "was permitted to remain by himself" | "was permitted to stay by himself" | "was allowed to live on his own" | "was permitted to stay by himself" (LOGOS) -- two-to-one on "permitted"; PNEUMA's "allowed to live on his own" is more paraphrastic. |
| 17 | Temporal phrasing | "it happened after three days" | "it came to pass that after three days" | "Three days later" | "it came to pass that after three days" (LOGOS) -- egeneto is a Lukan construction; preserving it honors the source. |
| 17 | Description of Jewish leaders | "those being the first [protous] of the Jews" | "the leading men of the Jews" | "the local Jewish leaders" | "the leading men of the Jews" (LOGOS) -- protous means "first/leading men." PNEUMA's "local" is an addition. |
| 17 | Address form | "Men, brothers [andres adelphoi]" | "Brothers" | "Brothers" | "Brothers" -- LOGOS and PNEUMA agree; EMET's "Men, brothers" is hyper-literal for andres adelphoi, which functions as a standard address. |
| 17 | "customs of our fathers/ancestors" | "the customs of-the-fathers" | "the customs of our fathers" | "the customs of our ancestors" | "the customs of our fathers" (LOGOS) -- tois patrois is "ancestral/of the fathers." PNEUMA's "ancestors" is acceptable but "fathers" is more traditional for patrois. |
| 17 | "delivered a prisoner" phrasing | "was delivered a prisoner out of Jerusalem" | "was delivered as a prisoner from Jerusalem" | "was handed over to the Romans as a prisoner from Jerusalem" | "was delivered as a prisoner from Jerusalem" (LOGOS) -- PNEUMA restructures and adds "to the Romans" earlier than the Greek has it. |
| 18 | Reason for release | "because not-one cause of death was in me" | "because there was no cause of death in me" | "because there was no basis for a death sentence against me" | "because there was no cause for a death sentence against me" -- blends LOGOS's structure with PNEUMA's clarifying "death sentence" (aitian thanatou). |
| 19 | "speaking-against" vs "objected" | "the Jews speaking-against [it]" | "when the Jews objected" | "when the Jewish leaders objected" | "when the Jews objected" (LOGOS) -- PNEUMA's "Jewish leaders" adds a word not in the text. |
| 19 | "compelled" vs "forced" | "I was compelled to appeal-to Caesar" | "I was compelled to appeal to Caesar" | "I was forced to appeal to Caesar" | "I was compelled to appeal to Caesar" -- two-to-one; enagkasthen is well rendered by "compelled." |
| 19 | "accusation" phrasing | "not as having anything to accuse my nation [of]" | "not as though I had any accusation to bring against my own nation" | "not because I had any charge to bring against my own nation" | Adopted LOGOS -- "not as though I had any accusation to bring against my own nation." More precise than PNEUMA's "charge" for kategorein. |
| 20 | Opening phrase | "For this reason, therefore, I called-for you" | "For this reason, therefore, I have called for you" | "This is the reason I asked to see you" | "For this reason, therefore, I have called for you" (LOGOS) -- preserves dia tauten oun ten aitian. PNEUMA restructures too freely. |
| 20 | Chain phrasing | "this chain I am-wearing" | "I am bound with this chain" | "I am bound with this chain" | "I am bound with this chain" -- two-to-one; perikeimai can mean "to be bound with" or "to wear around." |
| 21 | "received" vs tense | "We neither received letters" | "We have received no letters" | "We have received no letters" | "We have received no letters" -- two-to-one; the perfect tense suits the context. |
| 21 | "evil" vs "bad" | "speak anything evil concerning you" | "spoken anything evil about you" | "said anything bad about you" | "spoken anything evil about you" (LOGOS) -- poneron is "evil/wicked," not merely "bad." |
| 22 | Opening | "we consider-it-worthy to hear" | "we think it fitting to hear" | "we would like to hear" | "we think it fitting to hear" (LOGOS) -- axioumen is "we consider worthy/fitting." PNEUMA's "would like" weakens the formal sense. |
| 22 | "sect" description | "concerning this sect [haireseōs], it is known to us that everywhere it is spoken-against" | "concerning this sect, it is known to us that everywhere it is spoken against" | "all we know about this sect is that it is spoken against everywhere" | Adopted LOGOS's word order, which follows the Greek placement of pantachou. |
| 23 | Temporal clause | "having-appointed him a day, they came to him into the lodging, more [in number]" | "having appointed a day for him, they came to him at his lodging in greater numbers" | "they arranged a day and came to him at his lodging in greater numbers" | "having appointed a day for him, they came to him at his lodging in greater numbers" (LOGOS) -- preserves the participial structure. |
| 23 | Paul's teaching activity | "he was explaining, testifying-solemnly" | "He expounded the matter to them, testifying to the kingdom of God" | "he explained everything to them, testifying about the kingdom of God" | "He expounded the matter to them, testifying to the kingdom of God" (LOGOS) -- exetitheto means "he set forth/expounded." PNEUMA's "everything" is an addition. |
| 23 | "persuading" phrasing | "persuading them concerning Jesus" | "persuading them concerning Jesus" | "trying to persuade them about Jesus" | "persuading them concerning Jesus" -- two-to-one; the Greek participle peithon does not require "trying to." |
| 24 | Verb forms | "some indeed were being-persuaded... others were disbelieving" | "some were persuaded... others disbelieved" | "Some were persuaded... others refused to believe" | "some were persuaded... others disbelieved" (LOGOS) -- PNEUMA's "refused to believe" adds volitional emphasis beyond the Greek epistoun. |
| 25 | Manner of departure | "being in-disagreement toward one another, they were departing" | "being in disagreement with one another, they began to depart" | "They disagreed among themselves and began to leave" | "being in disagreement with one another, they began to depart" (LOGOS) -- preserves the participial structure. |
| 25 | "one word" qualifier | "Paul having-spoken one word" | "after Paul had spoken one word" | "after Paul had spoken one final word" | "after Paul had spoken one word" (LOGOS) -- PNEUMA's "final" is interpretive addition; the Greek has rhema hen ("one word"). |
| 25 | Adverb for Spirit speaking | "Well did the Holy Spirit speak" | "Rightly did the Holy Spirit speak" | "The Holy Spirit spoke the truth" | "Rightly did the Holy Spirit speak" (LOGOS) -- kalos means "well/rightly." PNEUMA's "spoke the truth" is a paraphrase. |
| 25 | "fathers" vs "ancestors" | "your fathers" | "your fathers" | "your ancestors" | "your fathers" -- two-to-one. |
| 26 | Quotation rendering | "By hearing you-will-hear and by-no-means understand, and seeing you-will-see and by-no-means perceive" | "By hearing you will hear and will by no means understand, and seeing you will see and will by no means perceive" | "You will hear and hear but never understand; you will look and look but never see" | Adopted LOGOS -- follows the LXX structure with "by hearing" (akoe) and "by no means" (ou me). PNEUMA's "hear and hear" loses the instrumental dative and "never" weakens ou me. |
| 27 | "heard heavily" vs "hard of hearing" | "with the ears they heard heavily" | "with their ears they have heard heavily" | "their ears are hard of hearing" | "with their ears they have heard heavily" (LOGOS) -- bareos ekousan is literally "heard heavily." PNEUMA paraphrases. |
| 27 | "turn back" vs "turn" | "they should-turn-back, and I-will-heal them" | "and turn, and I would heal them" | "and turn back, and I would heal them" | "and turn, and I would heal them" (LOGOS) -- epistrepsosi is simply "turn/turn back"; both are valid but LOGOS is more concise. EMET and PNEUMA include "back." |
| 28 | "salvation of God" phrasing | "to the Gentiles [ethnesin] was sent this salvation [sōtērion] of God" | "this salvation of God has been sent to the Gentiles" | "this salvation from God has been sent to the Gentiles" | "this salvation of God has been sent to the Gentiles" (LOGOS) -- tou theou is genitive "of God," not "from God." EMET's word order follows the Greek emphatic placement but is awkward in English. |
| 28 | "will hear" vs "will listen" | "they also will hear" | "they indeed will listen" | "and they will listen" | "they indeed will listen" -- LOGOS's "indeed" captures the emphatic kai, and "listen" (akousontai) conveys receptive hearing appropriate to the context. |
| 29 | Phrasing of departure | "the Jews departed, having much dispute among themselves" | "the Jews departed, having much dispute among themselves" | "the Jews left, arguing intensely among themselves" | "the Jews departed, having much dispute among themselves" -- two-to-one; PNEUMA's "arguing intensely" is interpretive for suzetsin pollen. |
| 30 | Temporal phrasing | "a whole two-years" | "two whole years" | "two full years" | "two whole years" (LOGOS) -- two-to-one on word order (LOGOS/PNEUMA both put modifier after "two"). |
| 30 | Living arrangement | "in [his] own rented-dwelling" | "in his own rented dwelling" | "in his own rented quarters" | "in his own rented dwelling" (LOGOS) -- misthomati is "rented place/dwelling." |
| 30 | Reception of visitors | "was receiving all those coming-in to him" | "he welcomed all who came to him" | "he welcomed everyone who came to see him" | "he welcomed all who came to him" (LOGOS) -- apedecheto means "welcomed/received." PNEUMA's "to see him" is an addition. |
| 31 | Opening participle | "preaching the kingdom of God" | "proclaiming the kingdom of God" | "proclaiming the kingdom of God" | "proclaiming the kingdom of God" -- two-to-one; kerussein means "to proclaim/herald," which "proclaiming" captures more precisely than "preaching." |
| 31 | "boldness" modifier | "with all boldness [parrēsias]" | "with all boldness" | "with complete boldness" | "with all boldness" -- two-to-one; meta pases parresias is literally "with all boldness." |
| 31 | Final word rendering | "unhindered [akōlytōs]" | "unhindered" | "without any hindrance" | "unhindered" -- two-to-one; the single adverb akolutos is best rendered as a single English word, preserving the emphatic brevity of Luke's final word. |

---

-- SOPHIA
